summary: Gruber analyzes Apple's 'Get a Mac' campaign and argues that its genius lies in framing the Mac not as a rival to Windows but as something categorically different — a brand-name product versus a generic commodity. He explains that Apple and Microsoft have an 'orthogonal' rivalry since Apple sells computers while Microsoft sells operating systems. The ads diminish Windows by treating it as just one unremarkable component of the PC, rather than attacking it directly. Gruber then argues that Microsoft's retaliatory 'I'm a PC' campaign backfires spectacularly by reinforcing Apple's framing — emphasizing that PCs are generic and ubiquitous rather than special.
key_points: Apple and Microsoft have an orthogonal rivalry: Apple sells computers, Microsoft sells operating systems, so they aren't truly direct competitors
The 'Get a Mac' ads frame the choice not as either/or between rivals but as 'special vs. regular' — Mac as brand name vs. PC as generic category
The ads diminish Windows by treating it as just one unremarkable element of the PC rather than making it the explicit target
Microsoft's 'I'm a PC' response reinforces Apple's message by emphasizing ubiquity and genericness rather than countering with a distinct brand identity
money_quotes: Apple's direct competition isn't Microsoft but instead PC makers who sell computers running Windows.
The framing instead is special vs. regular. Not Coke vs. Pepsi but Coke vs. 'soda'.
Windows is not the Mac's rival or competitor. It is the omnipresent homogenizer that weighs PC down.
Mac OS X and the software that runs on it is special. It is something that the Mac can never lose and which the PC can never have.
What makes Microsoft's new 'I'm a PC' commercials so jaw-droppingly bad is that they're not countering Apple's message, but instead they're reinforcing it.
Microsoft's new ads emphasize the same message as Apple's: that the Mac is the one and only brand-name computer in the world.
key_insight: Microsoft's 'I'm a PC' campaign inadvertently reinforced Apple's framing by celebrating ubiquity and genericness — the exact qualities Apple's ads used to diminish the PC.
